steps.title: Variables and Data Types
hints: Use `const` for values that never change and `let` for values that will be reassigned. Avoid `var`.
Template literals use backticks and `${expression}` to embed values: `` `Host: ${host}:${port}` ``

steps.title: Functions and Arrow Functions
hints: A named function: `function greet(name) { return 'Hello ' + name; }`. An arrow function equivalent: `const greet = (name) => 'Hello ' + name;`
Both forms work identically for most purposes. Arrow functions are preferred for short callbacks; named functions are clearer for top-level definitions.

steps.title: Objects and Destructuring
hints: Destructuring pulls named properties out of an object in one line: `const { host, port } = config;` — equivalent to `const host = config.host; const port = config.port;`
You can rename while destructuring: `const { stageNumber: stage } = config;` — the variable is named `stage` but reads `config.stageNumber`.

steps.title: Arrays and Iteration
hints: `forEach` runs a function for each item but returns nothing. `map` returns a new array of transformed items. `filter` returns a new array of items that pass a test. `find` returns the first matching item (or undefined).
All four take a callback: `elements.map(el => el.elmName)` returns an array of just the names.

steps.title: CommonJS Modules
hints: Export with `module.exports = { functionName };` at the bottom of the file. Import with `const { functionName } = require('./filename');` — omit the `.js` extension.
You can export a single value (`module.exports = myFunction`) or an object of multiple exports (`module.exports = { fn1, fn2 }`).

steps.title: Classes and the `this` Keyword
hints: Inside a class method, `this` refers to the instance. Store constructor arguments on `this` so other methods can access them: `this.config = config;`
Methods are defined directly in the class body — no `function` keyword, no comma between methods: `class Foo { bar() { return 42; } }`

steps.title: Promises and async/await
hints: Mark a function `async` and you can use `await` inside it. `await` pauses until the Promise resolves and gives you its value: `const result = await someAsyncCall();`
`util.promisify(fn)` wraps a Node.js callback-style function (last argument is `(err, result) => {}`) so it returns a Promise instead.

steps.title: Error Handling with try/catch
hints: Wrap `await` calls in `try { ... } catch (err) { ... }` — errors from rejected Promises are caught just like synchronous throws.
Re-throw with context: `throw new Error('Failed to list elements: ' + err.message)` — the original error message is preserved but a clearer label is added.

steps.title: Working with JSON
hints: `JSON.parse(string)` converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object. `JSON.stringify(object)` does the reverse. Both throw on invalid input.
Zowe CLI returns JSON when passed `--rfj`. The response shape is `{ data: [...] }` — so `JSON.parse(stdout).data` gives you the element array.
